def_en: A measure of how much excess return an investment earns per unit of risk taken: (return − risk-free rate) ÷ volatility. It lets a high-return but volatile strategy be compared fairly with a steady one — if the return was bought with reckless risk, the Sharpe ratio exposes it.

sources.chapter: Macro, Markets & Risk 宏观、市场与风险
投资组合管理（三级）Portfolio Management (Level III)

etymology: 以诺奖得主 William Sharpe 命名——把'回报'与'风险'压缩成一个可比数字，是绩效评估的通用语。

synonyms.sentence: Risk-adjusted return is the only honest way to rank a cautious fund against a wild one.
Return per unit of risk asks not how much you made but how much you risked to make it.

collocations: beat the benchmark on a risk-adjusted basis
example: A hedge fund boasting 30% returns can have a worse Sharpe ratio than a steady 8% fund — the eye-catching number hid enormous risk.

def_zh: 金融铁律:更高的预期回报只能通过承担更高的风险来获取，没有例外。任何声称'高回报、低风险'的东西，要么是骗局，要么隐藏着尚未显现的风险。它是评估一切投资承诺的第一道过滤器。
def_en: Finance's iron law: higher expected returns can only be earned by taking on higher risk, no exceptions. Anything promising 'high return, low risk' is either a fraud or hiding a risk that hasn't shown up yet. It is the first filter for evaluating any investment claim.

sources.chapter: Macro, Markets & Risk 宏观、市场与风险
投资组合管理（三级）Portfolio Management (Level III)

etymology: trade-off（取舍、权衡）——风险与回报之间必须'交换':想要更多回报，就得交出更多安全。

synonyms.sentence: 'No free lunch' is the risk-return trade-off stated for cynics.
The risk premium is the extra return that persuades you to bear the extra risk.

collocations: if it's too good to be true
example: A scheme promising 15% a year with no risk violates the risk-return trade-off — which is exactly how every Ponzi scheme advertises itself.

def_zh: 政府发行的借据，承诺按期付息、到期还本。发达国家国债被视为'无风险资产'，其收益率是一切资产定价的基准（无风险利率）。债券价格与利率反向:利率升，存量债券贬值——这就是久期风险。
def_en: IOUs issued by governments, promising periodic interest and repayment at maturity. Advanced-economy government bonds are treated as the 'risk-free asset', and their yield is the benchmark for pricing everything else (the risk-free rate). Bond prices move inversely to rates: when rates rise, existing bonds lose value — that is duration risk.

sources.chapter: Macro, Markets & Risk 宏观、市场与风险
权益与固定收益 Equity & Fixed Income

etymology: bond 源自古英语 bond（束缚、契约）——债券即'一纸还款契约';government bond 亦称 sovereign debt、treasuries、gilts。

synonyms.sentence: Sovereign debt is a government's promise, only as good as its willingness and ability to pay.
The risk-free asset is the yardstick against which every risky return is measured.

example: In a panic, investors dump stocks and buy government bonds — a flight to safety that drives bond prices up and yields down.

def_zh: 衡量债券价格对利率变动敏感度的指标——久期越长（现金流回收越远），利率每变动1%，价格波动越大。它把'利率风险'量化，解释了为何长期债券与远期现金流的成长股，在加息中受伤最重。
def_en: A measure of a bond's price sensitivity to interest-rate changes — the longer the duration (the further out its cash flows), the more its price swings per 1% rate move. It quantifies interest-rate risk and explains why long-dated bonds, and growth stocks with far-off cash flows, get hurt most when rates rise.

sources.chapter: Macro, Markets & Risk 宏观、市场与风险
权益与固定收益 Equity & Fixed Income

etymology: duration 源自拉丁 durare（持续）——久期即'现金流平均回收所需的时间长度',时间越长，利率敏感度越高。

synonyms.sentence: Interest-rate sensitivity is why a rate move that nicks a short bond can gut a long one.
Long-duration assets — 30-year bonds, unprofitable growth stocks — fall hardest when rates rise.

example: When rates jumped, a 30-year bond fell far more than a 2-year one — high duration means high sensitivity, the same force that crushed long-duration tech stocks.

def_zh: 赋予持有者在未来以约定价格买入（看涨/call）或卖出（看跌/put）标的资产的权利、而非义务的金融合约。用于对冲风险或杠杆投机。其价值由布莱克-斯科尔斯模型定价，核心变量是波动率——不确定性本身有价格。
def_en: Financial contracts giving the holder the right, not the obligation, to buy (a call) or sell (a put) an underlying asset at a set price in the future. Used to hedge risk or speculate with leverage. Their value is priced by the Black-Scholes model, whose key input is volatility — uncertainty itself has a price.

sources.chapter: Macro, Markets & Risk 宏观、市场与风险
衍生品与另类投资 Derivatives & Alternatives

etymology: option 源自拉丁 optare（选择）——期权即'一份可选择行使与否的权利'，与义务相对。

synonyms.sentence: Calls and puts let you bet on direction while capping what you can lose.
'The right, not the obligation' is the whole magic of an option.

example: An airline buys call options on jet fuel: if prices spike it exercises and caps its cost; if they fall it lets the option expire — the right, not the obligation.

def_zh: 价值'派生'于标的资产（股票、债券、商品、汇率、利率）的金融合约:期权、期货、远期、互换。用于对冲风险、价格发现或投机。它们能有效转移风险，也因高杠杆与不透明成为系统性危机的放大器（2008）。
def_en: Financial contracts whose value derives from an underlying asset (stocks, bonds, commodities, currencies, rates): options, futures, forwards, swaps. Used to hedge, discover prices or speculate. They transfer risk efficiently — and, through leverage and opacity, can amplify systemic crises (2008).

sources.chapter: Macro, Markets & Risk 宏观、市场与风险
衍生品与另类投资 Derivatives & Alternatives

etymology: derive 源自拉丁 derivare（引水、导出，rivus 溪流）——衍生品的价值'导流自'标的资产。

synonyms.sentence: Contingent claims pay off only if the specified future arrives.
Buffett called derivatives 'financial weapons of mass destruction' for their hidden, leveraged linkages.

example: Derivatives let a farmer lock in next year's crop price — but the same instruments, stacked with leverage, propagated the 2008 collapse across the globe.

def_zh: 约定在未来某日以今天锁定的价格买卖标的资产的合约——期货标准化、交易所交易，远期定制化、场外交易。生产者与用户用它锁定价格、对冲波动;投机者用它下注方向。大宗商品与利率风险管理的核心工具。
def_en: Contracts to buy or sell an asset on a future date at a price locked in today — futures are standardised and exchange-traded, forwards are customised and over-the-counter. Producers and users lock in prices and hedge volatility; speculators bet on direction. A core tool for managing commodity and interest-rate risk.

sources.chapter: Macro, Markets & Risk 宏观、市场与风险
衍生品与另类投资 Derivatives & Alternatives

etymology: future（未来）——在未来交割的合约；forward（向前）同理，二者结构相同而标准化程度不同。

synonyms.sentence: A forward contract is a private handshake on tomorrow's price.
Locking in the price trades the chance of a windfall for the certainty of a plan.

example: A copper miner sells futures to lock in today's price for next year's output — trading potential upside for the certainty its budget needs.

def_zh: 通过建立一个与现有风险敞口反向的头寸来抵消潜在损失——牺牲部分潜在收益换取确定性。航空对冲油价、出口商对冲汇率、农户对冲粮价。对冲是保险，不是投机:目标是消除风险而非赌博。
def_en: Offsetting a potential loss by taking a position that moves opposite to an existing exposure — giving up some upside for certainty. Airlines hedge fuel, exporters hedge currency, farmers hedge crop prices. Hedging is insurance, not speculation: the aim is to remove risk, not to bet on it.

sources.chapter: Macro, Markets & Risk 宏观、市场与风险
衍生品与另类投资 Derivatives & Alternatives

etymology: hedge 源自古英语 hecg（树篱、屏障）——对冲即'为风险筑一道篱笆';'hedge your bets'亦同源。

synonyms.sentence: Risk offsetting pairs every exposure with its mirror image.
A natural hedge arises when costs and revenues move in the same currency, cancelling out.

example: An exporter earning in dollars but paying costs in yen hedges the currency — locking in the exchange rate so a swing can't wipe out its margin.

def_zh: 1973年为期权定价的开创性公式——把期权价值表示为标的价格、行权价、时间、无风险利率与波动率的函数。它使衍生品市场得以规模化，是量化金融的奠基石;但其'波动率恒定、价格连续'的假设在危机中失效，酿成风险。
def_en: The 1973 breakthrough formula for pricing options — expressing an option's value as a function of the underlying price, strike, time, the risk-free rate and volatility. It let derivatives markets scale and founded quantitative finance; but its assumptions (constant volatility, continuous prices) break down in crises, with dangerous consequences.

sources.chapter: Macro, Markets & Risk 宏观、市场与风险
衍生品与另类投资 Derivatives & Alternatives

etymology: 以 Fischer Black 与 Myron Scholes 命名（Merton 同获诺奖）——把物理学的热扩散方程搬入金融定价。

synonyms.sentence: Option pricing theory turned a gambler's guess into an engineer's formula.
Implied volatility is the market's forecast of turbulence, read backward out of the option price.

example: Black-Scholes made options tradeable at scale — but its assumption of tame, continuous prices helped blow up a Nobel-laureate hedge fund when markets jumped.
nuance: 模型风险的经典教训:LTCM（长期资本管理，含两位布莱克-斯科尔斯诺奖得主）1998年因模型假设在极端行情下失效而崩溃——公式再优雅，也困于假设。

def_zh: 用心理学解释市场为何偏离理性:投资者受损失厌恶、锚定、羊群效应、过度自信、可得性偏差驱动，制造泡沫与恐慌。它挑战有效市场假说——价格并非总是理性，情绪会系统性地扭曲定价。
def_en: Using psychology to explain why markets deviate from rationality: investors are driven by loss aversion, anchoring, herding, overconfidence and availability bias, creating bubbles and panics. It challenges the efficient market hypothesis — prices are not always rational, and emotion distorts them systematically.

sources.chapter: Macro, Markets & Risk 宏观、市场与风险
投资组合管理（三级）Portfolio Management (Level III)

etymology: behavioural（行为的）——研究真实投资者的'行为'而非理性人假设;Kahneman、Thaler 等因此获诺奖。

synonyms.sentence: Investor psychology is why fear and greed price assets the model says they shouldn't.
A cognitive bias is a mental shortcut that saves time and occasionally loses fortunes.

example: Behavioural finance explains bubbles: herding and overconfidence drive prices far above value until fear reverses them just as violently.

def_zh: 卡尼曼与特沃斯基:人对损失的痛苦约为对等额收益快乐的两倍。它导致投资者过早卖出盈利、死扛亏损（处置效应），也解释了保险、促销、谈判中的诸多非理性——损失的阴影比收益的光芒更长。
def_en: Kahneman and Tversky: the pain of a loss is roughly twice the pleasure of an equivalent gain. It makes investors sell winners too early and cling to losers (the disposition effect), and explains much irrationality in insurance, promotions and negotiation — the shadow of a loss is longer than the glow of a gain.

sources.chapter: Macro, Markets & Risk 宏观、市场与风险
投资组合管理（三级）Portfolio Management (Level III)

etymology: loss（损失）+ aversion（厌恶，拉丁 aversus 转开）——本能地'转身避开'损失，其力度远超对收益的追逐。

synonyms.sentence: The disposition effect sells the winners and marries the losers.
Prospect theory replaced the rational actor with a real, loss-fearing human.

example: Loss aversion makes investors hold a falling stock for years to avoid 'realising' the loss — the pain of admitting it outweighs the logic of moving on.

def_zh: 人在判断时过度依赖最先接触到的信息（'锚'），后续估计围绕它调整而调整不足。谈判先开价者设锚、定价'原价划掉'设锚、估值锚定历史价格——一个任意数字就能系统性扭曲判断。
def_en: People over-rely on the first piece of information they encounter (the 'anchor'), adjusting subsequent estimates around it but insufficiently. The first offer in a negotiation sets an anchor, a struck-through 'original price' sets one, valuations anchor on historical prices — an arbitrary number systematically warps judgement.

sources.chapter: Macro, Markets & Risk 宏观、市场与风险

etymology: anchor（锚）——最先的信息像船锚一样'固定'了后续判断的位置，故名。

synonyms.sentence: The anchor point is the first number in the room, and it never fully leaves.
Reference dependence judges everything relative to a starting point, not on its merits.

example: A menu's expensive dish anchors your sense of price so the second-priciest feels reasonable — anchoring bias, monetised.

def_zh: 卡尼曼与特沃斯基的诺奖理论:人不按最终财富的绝对水平、而按相对于参照点的得失来做决策，且对损失比收益敏感、对小概率过度加权。它取代了'理性经济人'，成为行为经济学的理论核心。
def_en: Kahneman and Tversky's Nobel-winning theory: people decide not by absolute final wealth but by gains and losses relative to a reference point, weighting losses more than gains and over-weighting small probabilities. It replaced the 'rational economic man' as the theoretical core of behavioural economics.

sources.chapter: Macro, Markets & Risk 宏观、市场与风险

etymology: prospect（前景、可能的结果）——人评估的是各种'前景'相对参照点的得失，而非绝对财富。

synonyms.sentence: Reference-dependent choice means the same outcome delights or dismays depending on the baseline.
Probability weighting is why people buy both lottery tickets and insurance.

collocations: gains and losses vs final wealth
example: Prospect theory explains why the same person buys a lottery ticket and an insurance policy — over-weighting the tiny chances of a jackpot and a disaster alike.

def_zh: 塞勒与桑斯坦:通过设计'选择架构'（默认选项、排序、提示）在不限制自由、不改变经济激励的前提下，把人温和地推向更好的决策。默认自动加入养老金即经典案例——'自由家长主义'。
def_en: Thaler and Sunstein: by designing the 'choice architecture' (defaults, ordering, prompts), you can gently steer people toward better decisions without restricting freedom or changing economic incentives. Auto-enrolment in pensions is the classic case — 'libertarian paternalism'.

sources.chapter: Macro, Markets & Risk 宏观、市场与风险

etymology: nudge（轻推）——用'肘部轻碰'般的微小干预引导行为,而非命令或禁止。

synonyms.sentence: Choice architecture shapes decisions by arranging the options, not by removing any.
The default effect is the most powerful nudge: most people never change the setting they're given.

example: Switching pensions from opt-in to opt-out — a pure nudge — lifts enrolment dramatically, because inertia now works for saving instead of against it.

def_zh: 代表企业所有权份额的证券——股东分享利润（分红）与增值，但承担最末位、最高的风险。长期看，股票回报高于债券与现金（股权风险溢价），是财富增值的核心引擎，代价是短期高波动。
def_en: Securities representing ownership stakes in companies — shareholders share in profits (dividends) and appreciation, but bear the last, riskiest claim. Over the long run, equities out-return bonds and cash (the equity risk premium), the core engine of wealth-building, at the price of short-term volatility.

sources.chapter: Macro, Markets & Risk 宏观、市场与风险
权益与固定收益 Equity & Fixed Income

etymology: 见 cost-of-equity;equity 源自拉丁 aequitas（公平）——股票即'公平分享企业剩余'的凭证。

synonyms.sentence: Shares turn a company into something millions can own a sliver of.
Ownership capital is patient money that rides the whole journey, up and down.

example: Over decades equities have beaten bonds and cash by a wide margin — the reward, per the risk-return trade-off, for enduring their stomach-churning swings.

def_zh: 营销战略的核心三步:把市场细分为不同需求的群体（segmentation），选择要服务的目标群体（targeting），在目标顾客心智中确立独特位置（positioning）。它把'为所有人服务'的幻想变成'为谁、凭什么'的清晰选择。
def_en: Marketing strategy's core three steps: divide the market into groups with distinct needs (segmentation), choose which to serve (targeting), and establish a distinct place in the target customer's mind (positioning). It turns the fantasy of 'serving everyone' into the clear choice of 'for whom, and on what basis'.

sources.chapter: Marketing, Growth & Operations 营销、增长与运营

etymology: Segmentation/Targeting/Positioning 首字母缩略——科特勒使其成为现代营销战略的标准框架。

synonyms.sentence: Target marketing accepts that pleasing everyone means being chosen by no one.
Digital data pushes STP toward the segment of one — personalisation at scale.

example: STP forces the discipline of choice: a brand that segments, picks a target and positions sharply beats one that vaguely markets to 'everyone'.

def_zh: 营销执行的四大可控杠杆:产品（Product）、价格（Price）、渠道（Place）、促销（Promotion）。它们必须相互一致地支撑同一定位——高端产品配低价或劣质渠道，四要素打架，定位就崩了。服务业常扩展为7P。
def_en: The four controllable levers of marketing execution: Product, Price, Place, Promotion. They must reinforce one and the same positioning — a premium product with a cut price or a shabby channel is a mix at war with itself, and the positioning collapses. Services often extend it to 7Ps.

sources.chapter: Marketing, Growth & Operations 营销、增长与运营

etymology: McCarthy 1960年提出的 Product/Price/Place/Promotion 四要素——'4P'成为营销入门第一课。

synonyms.sentence: The four Ps are only as strong as their agreement with one another.
The go-to-market mix is where positioning either comes true or falls apart.

example: A luxury brand's marketing mix must cohere — premium product, high price, exclusive place, aspirational promotion; discount any one and the whole positioning cheapens.

def_zh: 品牌名字本身所承载的、超越产品功能的价值:知名度、认知质量、联想、忠诚。强品牌带来定价权、降低获客成本、抵御竞争与危机——它是资产负债表上看不见却最真实的护城河之一。
def_en: The value a brand name carries beyond the product's function: awareness, perceived quality, associations, loyalty. Strong brands command price premiums, lower acquisition costs, and buffer against competition and crises — an invisible line on the balance sheet and one of the most real moats there is.

sources.chapter: Marketing, Growth & Operations 营销、增长与运营

etymology: brand 源自古诺斯 brandr（烙印）——牧场主给牲畜'烙印'以示所有;品牌资产即这枚烙印积累的信任价值。

synonyms.sentence: Brand value is the premium customers pay for a promise they've learned to trust.
The intangible asset of a brand rarely appears on the balance sheet and often dwarfs it.

example: Brand equity lets a soft-drink maker charge more for sugar water — the liquid is a commodity, the brand is the asset.

def_zh: 里斯与特劳特:定位不是对产品做什么，而是对潜在顾客的心智做什么——在顾客脑中占据一个独特、简单、可防守的位置（'安全的沃尔沃''最快的联邦快递'）。心智容量有限，第一个占据某认知的品牌最有利。
def_en: Ries and Trout: positioning is not what you do to a product but what you do to the mind of the prospect — occupying a distinct, simple, defensible slot in the customer's head ('Volvo is safety', 'FedEx is overnight'). The mind has limited room, and the brand that owns a perception first has the edge.

sources.chapter: Marketing, Growth & Operations 营销、增长与运营

etymology: 见 competitive-positioning;营销学的 positioning 特指'在顾客心智中的位置',而非战略学的'活动组合'。

synonyms.sentence: Owning a word in the customer's mind is worth more than owning a factory.
A positioning statement fits on a napkin or it isn't sharp enough.

collocations: own a position in the mind
example: Volvo spent decades owning 'safety' in buyers' minds — positioning so strong that the word and the brand became nearly interchangeable.

def_zh: 一个客户在其与企业关系全周期内贡献的总利润现值。它是判断'为获取一个客户能花多少钱'的上限，与获客成本（CAC）之比决定商业模式是否成立——LTV 远大于 CAC 才能可持续盈利地增长。
def_en: The present value of all the profit a customer contributes over the whole life of the relationship. It sets the ceiling on what you can spend to acquire a customer; its ratio to acquisition cost (CAC) determines whether the model works — LTV must comfortably exceed CAC to grow profitably.

sources.chapter: Marketing, Growth & Operations 营销、增长与运营

etymology: lifetime（一生）——客户在'与品牌关系的一生'中创造的总价值;是订阅与电商时代的核心指标。

synonyms.sentence: Lifetime value tells you how much a customer is worth, not just what they spent today.
Customer equity is the whole customer base valued as an asset.

example: If a subscriber's LTV is 900 and costs 300 to acquire, the 3:1 LTV/CAC ratio says spend more to grow; flip it, and growth just burns cash.

def_zh: 获取一个新客户的全部平均成本:营销、销售、渠道、补贴总支出除以新增客户数。CAC 与客户终身价值（LTV）的关系是增长可持续性的核心检验;CAC 回收期越短，增长越健康、越不依赖持续输血。
def_en: The full average cost of winning one new customer: total marketing, sales, channel and subsidy spend divided by customers gained. Its relationship to lifetime value (LTV) is the core test of sustainable growth; the shorter the CAC payback period, the healthier the growth and the less it depends on constant funding.

sources.chapter: Marketing, Growth & Operations 营销、增长与运营

etymology: acquisition 源自拉丁 acquirere（获取）——获客成本即'把一个陌生人变成付费客户的代价'。

synonyms.sentence: Cost per acquisition rising while LTV stalls is a growth model quietly breaking.
The CAC payback period is how many months a customer takes to earn back what you spent to win them.

example: When a startup's CAC climbs as it exhausts cheap channels while LTV holds flat, the unit economics deteriorate even as revenue grows.

def_zh: 把用户从初次接触到最终购买的旅程画成逐级收窄的漏斗:认知→兴趣→考虑→意向→购买。每一级都有流失，优化的关键是找到流失最严重的一环集中改进——漏斗思维把增长变成可测量、可迭代的工程。
def_en: The user's journey from first contact to purchase, drawn as a narrowing funnel: awareness → interest → consideration → intent → purchase. Each stage leaks; the key to optimisation is finding the leakiest stage and fixing it first. Funnel thinking turns growth into a measurable, iterable engineering problem.

sources.chapter: Marketing, Growth & Operations 营销、增长与运营

etymology: funnel（漏斗）——用户群体从宽口（认知）逐级收窄到细口（购买），形似漏斗。

synonyms.sentence: The marketing funnel makes each drop-off a number you can attack.
Conversion rate optimisation compounds: a few points at each stage multiply through.

example: The funnel showed 40% of sign-ups never activated — fixing that single stage lifted revenue more than doubling ad spend would have.

def_zh: 增长框架把用户生命周期拆成五个可衡量阶段:获取（Acquisition）、激活（Activation）、留存（Retention）、推荐（Referral）、变现（Revenue）。逐段量化与优化，让'增长'从玄学变成数据驱动的系统工程。首字母连读像海盗喊'Aarrr'。
def_en: A growth framework splitting the user lifecycle into five measurable stages: Acquisition, Activation, Retention, Referral, Revenue. Quantifying and optimising each turns 'growth' from mystique into data-driven systems engineering. The initials read aloud sound like a pirate's 'Aarrr'.

sources.chapter: Marketing, Growth & Operations 营销、增长与运营

etymology: Dave McClure 提出的 Acquisition/Activation/Retention/Referral/Revenue——因缩写谐音海盗吼叫而称'pirate metrics'。

synonyms.sentence: The growth funnel measures a user from first click to loyal advocate.
Lifecycle metrics reveal whether you have a growth problem or a retention one.

example: AARRR revealed the real problem wasn't acquisition but retention — a leaky bucket no amount of top-of-funnel spend could fill.

def_zh: 流失率是某期内流失客户占比,留存率是其反面。对订阅制企业,留存是生死线:高流失如漏水的桶,再多获客也留不住增长。改善留存往往比获取新客更省钱、更能提升终身价值——留存是增长的复利引擎。
def_en: Churn is the share of customers lost in a period; retention is its mirror image. For subscription businesses, retention is existential: high churn is a leaky bucket no amount of acquisition can fill. Improving retention is usually cheaper than acquiring new customers and lifts lifetime value more — retention is growth's compounding engine.

sources.chapter: Marketing, Growth & Operations 营销、增长与运营

etymology: churn 源自搅拌牛奶的'搅动'——客户'搅动流失';retention 源自拉丁 retinere（保持）。

synonyms.sentence: The leaky bucket is why acquisition without retention is pouring water into sand.
Net revenue retention above 100% means you grow even with the front door shut.

example: At 5% monthly churn, a company loses half its customers a year — retention, not acquisition, is the leak that decides whether it grows or drains.

def_zh: 把用户按加入时间（或其他共同特征）分组，追踪各组随时间的行为（留存、消费、活跃）。它剥开总量增长的表象:总用户数可能在涨，而每一批新用户的留存却在恶化——队列分析揭示商业模式的真实健康度。
def_en: Grouping users by when they joined (or another shared trait) and tracking each group's behaviour over time (retention, spend, activity). It cuts through headline growth: total users may rise while each new cohort retains worse — cohort analysis reveals a business model's true health.

sources.chapter: Marketing, Growth & Operations 营销、增长与运营

etymology: cohort 源自拉丁 cohors（罗马军团的一个步兵队）——引申为'一起行动的一群人';按时间分批追踪。

synonyms.sentence: Retention curves by cohort expose whether the product is getting better or worse over time.
Vintage analysis, borrowed from lending, tracks each batch's performance separately.

example: Aggregate users grew, but cohort analysis showed each new batch churning faster — the growth was masking a deteriorating product-market fit.

def_zh: 定价是利润最直接的杠杆——价格提升1%对利润的影响通常远超销量提升1%。三大方法:成本加成（安全但漠视价值）、竞争导向（随行就市）、价值导向（按客户感知价值定价，最能获取价值）。定价权本身即竞争优势的体现。
def_en: Price is the most direct lever on profit — a 1% price rise typically lifts profit far more than a 1% volume rise. The three approaches: cost-plus (safe but blind to value), competition-based (matching the market), and value-based (pricing to the customer's perceived value, which captures the most). Pricing power is itself the mark of competitive advantage.

sources.chapter: Marketing, Growth & Operations 营销、增长与运营

etymology: price 源自拉丁 pretium（价值、价格）——定价即'为价值标定一个数字',与 prize、appreciate 同源。

synonyms.sentence: Value-based pricing charges for the outcome, not the cost of production.
Price is the profit lever with the shortest arm and the heaviest load.

example: Cost-plus pricing leaves money on the table; value-based pricing asks what the outcome is worth to the customer — and captures a share of it.

def_zh: 向不同客户、为相同或相似产品收取不同价格，以攫取更多消费者剩余——学生票、商务舱、区域定价、批量折扣、动态定价。它需要能区分客户支付意愿并防止套利。是提升利润最有力却最需精细设计的工具。
def_en: Charging different customers different prices for the same or similar product, to capture more consumer surplus — student tickets, business class, regional pricing, volume discounts, dynamic pricing. It requires being able to distinguish willingness to pay and prevent arbitrage. A powerful profit lever that demands careful design.

sources.chapter: Marketing, Growth & Operations 营销、增长与运营

etymology: discriminate 源自拉丁 discriminare（区分）——价格歧视即'按客户区别定价',经济学中为中性术语，无贬义。

synonyms.sentence: Differential pricing lets a firm sell to the frugal without giving the rich a discount.
Consumer surplus capture is the polite academic name for charging what each buyer will bear.

example: Airlines are masters of price discrimination — the same seat sells at ten prices depending on when, who and how flexibly you book.

def_zh: 客户为一件产品愿意支付的最高价格——价值的主观上限。它是价值定价的锚:定价应逼近而非远低于 WTP。客户剩余=WTP−实付价格;企业攫取的价值=实付价格−成本。理解 WTP 分布是定价艺术的核心。
def_en: The maximum a customer would pay for a product — the subjective ceiling of value. It is the anchor of value-based pricing: set price near, not far below, WTP. Consumer surplus = WTP − price paid; the value the firm captures = price − cost. Understanding the distribution of WTP is the heart of the pricing art.

sources.chapter: Marketing, Growth & Operations 营销、增长与运营

etymology: 字面'愿意支付'——经济学用它衡量客户对产品的主观估值上限,与'reservation price'（保留价格）同义。

synonyms.sentence: The reservation price is the wall past which the customer walks away.
Perceived value, not cost, is the true ceiling on what you can charge.

example: Knowing that business travellers have far higher willingness to pay than tourists, airlines fence them apart and price each near its own ceiling.

def_zh: 自我强化的增长机制:产出（新用户、内容、收入）被再投入为下一轮增长的输入，形成复利飞轮，而非线性的漏斗。病毒循环（用户带用户）、内容循环、付费再投资循环是主要类型。好的增长循环让增长内生、可复利。
def_en: Self-reinforcing growth mechanisms where the output (new users, content, revenue) is reinvested as the input to the next round of growth — a compounding flywheel rather than a linear funnel. Viral loops (users bring users), content loops and paid-reinvestment loops are the main types. A good growth loop makes growth endogenous and compounding.

sources.chapter: Marketing, Growth & Operations 营销、增长与运营

etymology: loop（循环）——增长的产出'循环'回流为输入,与一次性的 funnel（漏斗）相对。

synonyms.sentence: A flywheel takes effort to start and then turns increasingly by its own momentum.
A viral loop makes each user a recruiter of the next.

example: A referral program is a growth loop: each new user invites others, whose sign-ups fund and fuel the next wave — growth that feeds itself.

def_zh: 衡量用户自发带来新用户的能力:K因子=每个用户平均发出的邀请数×邀请转化率。K>1 意味着指数式自增长（每个用户带来不止一个新用户），是零获客成本增长的圣杯，但极少数产品能持续实现。
def_en: A measure of how effectively users spontaneously bring in new users: the K-factor = invitations sent per user × conversion rate of those invitations. K > 1 means exponential self-growth (each user brings more than one), the holy grail of zero-CAC growth — which very few products sustain.

sources.chapter: Marketing, Growth & Operations 营销、增长与运营

etymology: viral 源自 virus（拉丁'毒液、黏液'）——传播如病毒般人传人;K因子借自流行病学的基本传染数 R0。

synonyms.sentence: The viral coefficient decides whether growth ignites or fizzles.
Word of mouth is virality that predates the spreadsheet.

example: A messaging app with a K-factor above 1 grows exponentially without ad spend — each user recruits more than one, and the base doubles on itself.

def_zh: 用一个问题衡量客户忠诚:'你有多大可能把我们推荐给朋友?'（0–10分）。推荐者（9–10）占比减去贬损者（0–6）占比即 NPS。简单、可比、与增长相关，但也因过度简化、易被操纵而受批评。
def_en: Measuring loyalty with one question: 'how likely are you to recommend us to a friend?' (0–10). The share of promoters (9–10) minus the share of detractors (0–6) is the NPS. Simple, comparable and correlated with growth — though criticised for over-simplifying and being easy to game.

sources.chapter: Marketing, Growth & Operations 营销、增长与运营

etymology: Fred Reichheld 2003年提出——'net'指推荐者减贬损者的净值;因其单一问题的简洁而风行企业界。

synonyms.sentence: Promoters minus detractors reduces loyalty to one blunt, comparable number.
Reichheld called 'would you recommend us?' the ultimate question.

example: A rising NPS often precedes a rising customer base — happy customers become an unpaid, credible sales force through referrals.
nuance: NPS 批评:单一指标掩盖复杂真相,且易被员工'求高分'操纵;宜作趋势而非绝对值,并追问'为什么'的定性反馈。

def_zh: 丰田开创的生产哲学:系统性消除一切不增值的浪费（muda）——过度生产、等待、搬运、库存、多余动作、缺陷、过度加工。核心是准时制（JIT）、持续改善（kaizen）与对流程的尊重。它重塑了全球制造业。
def_en: Toyota's production philosophy: systematically eliminate all non-value-adding waste (muda) — overproduction, waiting, transport, inventory, motion, defects, over-processing. Its core is just-in-time (JIT), continuous improvement (kaizen) and respect for the process. It reshaped global manufacturing.

sources.chapter: Marketing, Growth & Operations 营销、增长与运营

etymology: lean（精瘦的）——MIT 学者1980年代为丰田体系命名,喻'去除一切赘肉的生产';muda（无駄）日语'浪费'。

synonyms.sentence: The Toyota Production System turned waste elimination into a competitive weapon.
Just-in-time makes inventory arrive exactly when needed, and never sooner.

example: Lean manufacturing let Toyota hold minimal inventory and catch defects at source — quality and cost advantages Western rivals took decades to copy.
nuance: 精益的准时制（JIT）以极低库存换效率,但2020–22年供应链危机暴露其脆弱:一环断裂,全线停摆——效率与韧性的经典权衡。

def_zh: 日语'改善':通过全员参与、日积月累的微小改进实现质量与效率的持续提升——不追求一次性革命，而信奉复利式的渐进。它把改进从管理层的项目变成每个一线员工的日常习惯。
def_en: Japanese for 'change for better': continuous, incremental improvement through everyone's participation, compounding small gains in quality and efficiency — not one-off revolution but faith in gradual, compounding progress. It makes improvement a daily habit of every frontline worker, not a management project.

sources.chapter: Marketing, Growth & Operations 营销、增长与运营

etymology: 日语 改善 = kai（改变）+ zen（好）——'向好的改变';丰田体系使其成为全球质量管理通用词。

synonyms.sentence: Continuous improvement compounds like interest: small gains, relentlessly repeated.
The marginal-gains philosophy wins by improving a hundred things by 1% each.

example: Kaizen means the assembly-line worker who spots a better way changes it today — improvement as a daily reflex, not an annual initiative.

def_zh: 精益生产的核心:只在需要的时刻、按需要的数量生产或采购,把库存降到最低。它释放被库存占用的资金、暴露流程问题、逼迫质量,但以低冗余换效率,对供应中断极为敏感——效率与韧性的取舍。
def_en: The core of lean production: produce or procure only what is needed, when needed, in the amount needed, driving inventory to a minimum. It frees up cash tied in stock, exposes process problems and forces quality — but trades slack for efficiency, leaving it acutely vulnerable to supply disruption. Efficiency versus resilience.

sources.chapter: Marketing, Growth & Operations 营销、增长与运营

etymology: just in time（恰好及时）——零部件'恰好在需要时'到达生产线,而非提前堆积成库存。

synonyms.sentence: Lean inventory is cash freed from the warehouse and put back to work.
Pull production makes demand summon supply, not the other way round.

example: Just-in-time let manufacturers run on almost no inventory — a triumph of efficiency until a single chip shortage idled entire car plants.

def_zh: 以数据与统计驱动的质量管理方法,目标是把缺陷率降到每百万次机会不超过3.4次（六个标准差）。核心流程 DMAIC:定义-测量-分析-改进-控制。它把质量从主观判断变成可测量、可控制的统计工程。
def_en: A data- and statistics-driven quality methodology aiming to cut defects to no more than 3.4 per million opportunities (six standard deviations). Its core process is DMAIC: Define, Measure, Analyse, Improve, Control. It turns quality from subjective judgement into measurable, controllable statistical engineering.

sources.chapter: Marketing, Growth & Operations 营销、增长与运营

etymology: sigma（希腊字母 σ）是统计中标准差的符号——'六西格玛'即质量控制在正负六个标准差内,缺陷率极低;摩托罗拉与 GE 使其闻名。

synonyms.sentence: DMAIC gives improvement a disciplined, repeatable spine.
Defect reduction at Six Sigma levels means near-perfect, measured to the decimal.

example: Six Sigma attacks variation: by measuring and controlling every source of defect, a process approaches near-perfection instead of merely 'good enough'.
nuance: 批评:六西格玛擅长优化现有流程,却可能扼杀创新（过度控制变异也压制探索）——3M 引入后创新受挫是著名反例。

def_zh: 高德拉特《目标》:任何系统的产出都受制于其最弱环节（瓶颈/约束）。改进非瓶颈环节毫无意义,唯有识别并突破瓶颈才能提升整体产出。'一条链的强度取决于最弱的一环'——聚焦约束,是运营优化的杠杆点。
def_en: Goldratt's The Goal: a system's output is limited by its weakest link (the bottleneck or constraint). Improving non-bottlenecks is pointless; only identifying and breaking the constraint raises total throughput. 'A chain is only as strong as its weakest link' — focusing on the constraint is the leverage point of operations.

sources.chapter: Marketing, Growth & Operations 营销、增长与运营

etymology: constraint 源自拉丁 constringere（束紧）——约束即'捆住系统产出的那一环';Goldratt 以小说《目标》普及此理论。

synonyms.sentence: The bottleneck governs the flow; everything upstream of it just piles up.
Throughput thinking optimises the whole, not the sum of the parts.

example: The theory of constraints says speeding up every machine but the bottleneck just grows a bigger pile of work-in-progress in front of it.

def_zh: 限制整个流程产能的最狭窄环节——如瓶子的颈部限制倒出速度。系统的最大产出永远等于瓶颈的产出;非瓶颈环节的闲置或加速都不改变全局。找到并扩宽瓶颈,是提升产出最有效的单点干预。
def_en: The narrowest stage that limits a whole process's capacity — like a bottle's neck limiting the pour. A system's maximum output always equals the bottleneck's output; idle time or speed-ups elsewhere change nothing. Finding and widening the bottleneck is the single most effective intervention to raise output.

sources.chapter: Marketing, Growth & Operations 营销、增长与运营

etymology: bottle（瓶）+ neck（颈）——瓶颈限制流出速度,喻限制系统产能的最窄环节。

synonyms.sentence: The constraint is where an hour lost is an hour lost for the entire system.
The rate-limiting step, borrowed from chemistry, sets the pace of the whole reaction.

example: An hour of downtime at the bottleneck is an hour of lost output for the entire factory — which is why it gets the maintenance priority.

def_zh: 从原材料到成品交付给终端客户所经历的全部组织、活动、信息与资源的网络。管理它是在成本、速度、可靠性与韧性之间求平衡。全球化把供应链拉得又长又薄,效率极高却极脆弱——'即时'与'以防万一'的世纪之争。
def_en: The whole network of organisations, activities, information and resources that carries a product from raw material to the end customer. Managing it means balancing cost, speed, reliability and resilience. Globalisation stretched supply chains long and thin — hyper-efficient yet fragile — reigniting the century's debate between 'just-in-time' and 'just-in-case'.

sources.chapter: Marketing, Growth & Operations 营销、增长与运营

etymology: supply（供应)+ chain（链）——从源头到终端环环相扣的'供应之链';每一环的中断都会沿链传导。

synonyms.sentence: The value network is the supply chain seen as a web, not a line.
Just-in-case resilience buys buffers that just-in-time efficiency sold off.

example: The pandemic taught firms that a supply chain optimised only for cost is a liability — pushing a shift from just-in-time toward just-in-case buffers.

def_zh: 终端需求的微小波动，沿供应链向上游逐级放大——零售端销量小幅变化,会在批发、制造、原材料端引发越来越剧烈的订单摆动。源于信息滞后、批量订货与预期博弈。它是库存过剩与短缺周期的隐形推手。
def_en: Small fluctuations in end demand amplify as they travel upstream along the supply chain — a minor change in retail sales triggers ever-wilder order swings at wholesale, manufacturing and raw-material stages. Caused by information lag, batch ordering and expectation games. It is the hidden driver of glut-and-shortage cycles.

sources.chapter: Marketing, Growth & Operations 营销、增长与运营

etymology: bullwhip（牛鞭）——手腕的微小动作在鞭梢放大为剧烈甩动,喻需求波动沿供应链的逐级放大。

synonyms.sentence: Demand amplification turns a ripple at the till into a tidal wave at the factory.
The whiplash effect is why suppliers swing between famine and flood.

example: A 5% dip in retail sales became a 40% collapse in factory orders — the bullwhip effect, as each tier over-corrected on stale information.

def_zh: 排队论的基本定律:系统中平均在制品数量=平均到达率×平均停留时间（L=λW）。它简洁地连接了库存、吞吐与周期时间——想缩短交付时间,要么提高吞吐,要么减少在制品。运营与服务设计的通用工具。
def_en: A fundamental law of queueing theory: the average number of items in a system = average arrival rate × average time in the system (L = λW). It elegantly links inventory, throughput and cycle time — to shorten lead time, either raise throughput or cut work-in-progress. A universal tool of operations and service design.

sources.chapter: Marketing, Growth & Operations 营销、增长与运营

etymology: 以数学家 John Little（1961年证明）命名——排队论中最简洁而普适的恒等式。

synonyms.sentence: The queueing identity holds whether you're managing a factory, a hospital or a to-do list.
Work-in-progress limits use Little's law to cut cycle time by cutting the queue.

example: Little's law explains why a team drowning in half-finished tasks delivers slowly: cut the work-in-progress and, at the same throughput, everything finishes faster.

def_zh: 把异质的市场按需求、行为、人口或心理特征划分为内部相似、彼此不同的群体,以便差异化地设计产品、定价与沟通。有效细分的标准:可衡量、足够大、可触达、需求确有差异、可据以行动。
def_en: Dividing a heterogeneous market into groups that are similar within and different between, by need, behaviour, demographics or psychographics, so products, pricing and messaging can be tailored. Effective segments are measurable, substantial, reachable, genuinely different in need, and actionable.

sources.chapter: Marketing, Growth & Operations 营销、增长与运营

etymology: segment 源自拉丁 secare（切割）——细分即'把市场切成若干需求相近的块'。

synonyms.sentence: Customer segmentation admits that 'the average customer' is a fiction no one belongs to.
Needs-based segments beat demographic ones: what people want predicts more than who they are.

example: Segmenting by need rather than age revealed that budget and premium buyers cut across every demographic — reshaping the whole product line.

def_zh: 企业通过何种路径把产品送到客户手中:直销、经销、零售、电商、平台、加盟。渠道决定成本、控制力、客户关系与利润分配。直销掌控体验与数据但重资产,分销扩面快但让渡利润与客户关系——DTC 与平台正在重塑这场取舍。
def_en: The paths by which a firm gets its product to customers: direct sales, distributors, retail, e-commerce, platforms, franchising. Channel choice determines cost, control, customer relationship and margin split. Direct control owns experience and data but is asset-heavy; distribution scales reach but cedes margin and the customer relationship — a trade-off DTC and platforms are reshaping.

sources.chapter: Marketing, Growth & Operations 营销、增长与运营

etymology: channel 源自拉丁 canalis（水道）——渠道即'产品流向客户的水道';route to market 是其同义表达。

synonyms.sentence: The route to market decides who owns the customer relationship at the end of it.
Direct-to-consumer trades the retailer's reach for the brand's own data and margin.

example: Going direct-to-consumer lets a brand own the customer data and full margin — at the cost of building the logistics that retailers used to provide.

def_zh: 客户与品牌互动的全过程,从认知、考虑、购买到使用、复购、倡导——覆盖所有接触点（广告、网站、门店、客服、产品本身）。绘制旅程能发现痛点与'关键时刻',让体验设计从零散优化转向端到端的系统改进。
def_en: The full arc of a customer's interaction with a brand — from awareness, consideration and purchase to use, repurchase and advocacy — across every touchpoint (ads, website, store, support, the product itself). Mapping the journey surfaces pain points and 'moments of truth', shifting experience design from scattered fixes to end-to-end improvement.

sources.chapter: Marketing, Growth & Operations 营销、增长与运营

etymology: journey 源自古法语 journee（一天的行程）——客户'旅程'即其与品牌关系随时间展开的路径。

synonyms.sentence: The touchpoints are where a brand keeps or breaks its promise, one at a time.
Moments of truth are the few interactions that decide the whole relationship.

example: Mapping the customer journey revealed the worst friction wasn't in the product but in returns — fixing that one moment of truth lifted loyalty more than any feature.

def_zh: 把产品成功推向市场的整体计划:目标客户是谁、价值主张、定价、销售与营销渠道、以及销售运动（产品驱动、销售驱动或市场驱动）。GTM 常比产品本身更决定成败——同样的产品,错的 GTM 会静默失败。
def_en: The overall plan for bringing a product to market: who the target customer is, the value proposition, pricing, the sales and marketing channels, and the motion (product-led, sales-led or marketing-led). GTM often decides success more than the product itself — the same product with the wrong GTM fails quietly.

sources.chapter: Marketing, Growth & Operations 营销、增长与运营

etymology: go to market（走向市场）——把产品'推向市场'的完整策略;缩写 GTM 是 SaaS 与 B2B 的核心词。

synonyms.sentence: The GTM motion — product-led or sales-led — shapes the whole company around it.
Product-led growth lets the product do the selling before a salesperson calls.

example: A great product with a mismatched go-to-market — enterprise sales for a self-serve product — burns cash and stalls, while the right GTM would have compounded.

def_zh: 戴明等人开创的管理哲学:质量是全员、全流程、全周期的持续追求,而非事后检验。核心信念——质量提升同时降低成本（返工与浪费减少）,并以顾客满意为终极标尺。它奠定了日本制造的崛起。
def_en: A management philosophy from Deming and others: quality is a continuous pursuit by everyone, across every process and stage — not inspection after the fact. Its core belief is that improving quality lowers cost (less rework and waste) while customer satisfaction is the ultimate yardstick. It underpinned the rise of Japanese manufacturing.

sources.chapter: Marketing, Growth & Operations 营销、增长与运营

etymology: total（全面的）——质量是'全员、全过程'之事;Deming 的思想在战后日本落地,反哺全球。

synonyms.sentence: Quality at the source builds it in rather than inspecting it in.
The Deming cycle — plan, do, check, act — makes improvement never-ending.

example: TQM's insight overturned intuition: chasing quality lowered costs, because the cheapest defect is the one never made rather than the one caught and fixed.

def_zh: 商业模式从'一次性卖产品'转向'持续卖服务与关系':软件、媒体、消费品乃至汽车都在订阅化。它把不稳定的一次性交易变成可预测的经常性收入,但也把竞争焦点从'成交'永久转向'留存'——每月都要重新赢得客户。
def_en: The shift from selling a product once to selling an ongoing service and relationship: software, media, consumer goods, even cars are becoming subscriptions. It converts lumpy one-off sales into predictable recurring revenue — but permanently shifts the competitive focus from closing to retaining, re-winning the customer every month.

sources.chapter: Marketing, Growth & Operations 营销、增长与运营

etymology: subscribe 源自拉丁 sub（在下）+ scribere（写）——'在下方签名'订约;订阅经济即'以持续订约取代一次买断'。

synonyms.sentence: A recurring-revenue model earns the customer's money and their renewal, again and again.
Everything-as-a-service rents access where firms once sold ownership.

example: Software's move to subscription turned volatile licence sales into smooth recurring revenue — and made customer success, not the sales close, the priority.

def_zh: 如何划分职责、配置决策权、设计汇报关系与协调机制,使组织能高效执行战略。核心张力是分工带来的专业化与协调带来的整合之间的平衡。'结构追随战略'——组织设计必须服务于要赢的仗,而非相反。
def_en: How to divide responsibilities, allocate decision rights, and design reporting lines and coordination so the organisation can execute strategy effectively. The core tension balances specialisation from division of labour against integration from coordination. 'Structure follows strategy' — design must serve the battle you mean to win, not the reverse.

sources.chapter: Organisation, Tech & Governance 组织、科技与治理

etymology: organise 源自希腊 organon（工具、器官）——组织即'把人协调成一个能行动的有机体';设计即安排其结构。

synonyms.sentence: 'Structure follows strategy' is Chandler's warning against organising for the war you last fought.
The operating model is where strategy either gains a body or stays a slide.

example: A firm that centralises decisions for control but competes on local speed has an organisational design at war with its strategy — and loses to both.

def_zh: 一名管理者直接管辖的下属人数。幅度窄则层级多、控制紧但反应慢、成本高;幅度宽则层级少、更扁平灵活但需更强的授权与自治。它直接决定组织的层级数与沟通效率。
def_en: The number of subordinates a manager directly oversees. A narrow span means many layers — tight control but slow, costly; a wide span means fewer layers — flatter and nimbler but demanding more delegation and autonomy. It directly sets an organisation's number of levels and its communication efficiency.

sources.chapter: Organisation, Tech & Governance 组织、科技与治理

etymology: span（跨度）——一名管理者能'跨管'多少人;军队与教会最早研究此问题。

synonyms.sentence: Cutting management layers by widening the span speeds decisions and thins the payroll.
Delayering flattens the hierarchy on the bet that people manage themselves.

example: Widening the span of control from 5 to 9 let the company delete an entire management layer — faster decisions, lower cost, more autonomy.

def_zh: 员工同时向两条线汇报（如职能线与产品/地区线）的结构,以兼顾专业深度与跨部门协调。优点是灵活、资源共享;代价是双重汇报的权责模糊、冲突与政治。'谁说了算'的问题若不厘清,矩阵会陷入瘫痪。
def_en: A structure where employees report along two lines at once (e.g. function and product/region) to combine deep expertise with cross-cutting coordination. The upside is flexibility and shared resources; the cost is the ambiguity, conflict and politics of dual reporting. Unless 'who decides' is made clear, the matrix stalls.

sources.chapter: Organisation, Tech & Governance 组织、科技与治理

etymology: matrix 源自拉丁 matrix（母体、子宫，mater 母）——引申为行列交叉的网格;组织的两条汇报线交织成'矩阵'。

synonyms.sentence: Dual reporting doubles the coordination and, without clear rules, the confusion.
The two-boss problem is the matrix's chronic ailment: whose priority wins today?

example: In a matrix organisation an engineer answers to both a functional and a project lead — great for sharing scarce talent, fraught when their priorities collide.

def_zh: 明确规定'谁有权做哪类决策'——组织效能最被低估的杠杆之一。决策权模糊导致反复扯皮、拖延与推诿;清晰的决策权（配合 RACI、RAPID 等框架）让组织快速行动、责任到人。结构调整常不如厘清决策权见效。
def_en: Explicitly specifying who has authority over which decisions — one of the most underrated levers of organisational effectiveness. Ambiguous decision rights breed relitigation, delay and buck-passing; clear ones (with frameworks like RACI or RAPID) let an organisation move fast with real accountability. Clarifying them often beats restructuring.

sources.chapter: Organisation, Tech & Governance 组织、科技与治理

etymology: right（权利）源自古英语 riht（正、应得）——决策权即'正当拥有作某决定的权利'。

synonyms.sentence: The RAPID framework names who recommends, agrees, performs, inputs and decides.
Accountability without decision rights is blame without power.

example: A company sped up dramatically not by reorganising but by clarifying decision rights — ending the meetings whose only purpose was reopening settled calls.

def_zh: 当一方（代理人,如经理人）代表另一方（委托人,如股东）行事,但两者利益不一致、且委托人无法完全监督代理人时产生的冲突。经理人可能追求规模、薪酬或安逸而非股东价值。公司治理与激励设计的核心问题。
def_en: The conflict that arises when one party (the agent, e.g. a manager) acts for another (the principal, e.g. shareholders), but their interests diverge and the principal cannot fully monitor the agent. Managers may chase size, pay or a quiet life over shareholder value. It is the central problem of corporate governance and incentive design.

sources.chapter: Organisation, Tech & Governance 组织、科技与治理

etymology: agent 源自拉丁 agere（做、行动）——代理人即'代人行动者';principal 源自 princeps（首要者），即委托人。

synonyms.sentence: The agency cost is the value lost when the agent serves himself before the owner.
Skin in the game aligns the agent by making him a principal too.

example: Stock options try to solve the agency problem by making managers part-owners — so building shareholder value becomes building their own wealth.

def_zh: 引导与制衡公司管理层、使其对股东与利益相关者负责的制度体系:董事会、股东权利、审计、薪酬、信息披露、控制权市场。好的治理约束代理问题,坏的治理放任内部人掏空。它是资本市场信任的制度地基。
def_en: The system of institutions that directs and checks a company's management, holding it accountable to shareholders and stakeholders: the board, shareholder rights, audit, remuneration, disclosure, the market for control. Good governance restrains the agency problem; bad governance lets insiders loot. It is the institutional bedrock of trust in capital markets.

sources.chapter: Organisation, Tech & Governance 组织、科技与治理

etymology: govern 源自希腊 kybernan（掌舵）——治理即'为公司掌舵并设置制衡',与 cybernetics 同舵。

synonyms.sentence: Corporate checks and balances keep the people who run the company from owning it in all but name.
Board oversight is the shareholders' proxy in the room where they cannot sit.

example: Weak corporate governance — a captured board, no independent directors — is how a charismatic founder can burn shareholders' money for years unchecked.

def_zh: 企业应为谁服务的根本之争:股东至上论（弗里德曼:企业的社会责任就是增加利润）vs 利益相关者论（企业应平衡股东、员工、客户、社区、环境的利益）。ESG 浪潮把这场半世纪的辩论重新推上台前。
def_en: The fundamental dispute over whom a company serves: shareholder primacy (Friedman: the social responsibility of business is to increase profits) versus stakeholder capitalism (balancing shareholders, employees, customers, communities and the environment). The ESG wave has pushed this half-century debate back to centre stage.

sources.chapter: Organisation, Tech & Governance 组织、科技与治理

etymology: stakeholder（利益相关方）——原指'持赌注者';1960年代引申为'在企业中有利害关系的各方',与 shareholder（股东）对举。

synonyms.sentence: The Friedman doctrine says a manager spending shareholders' money on social causes is taxing without consent.
Shareholder primacy makes one interest sovereign and calls it clarity.

example: The stakeholder-versus-shareholder debate is not academic: it decides whether a board may sacrifice this quarter's profit for a decade's reputation.

def_zh: 引导组织与人员从现状过渡到目标状态的系统方法。科特八步:建立紧迫感、组建联盟、树立愿景、沟通、授权行动、创造短期胜利、巩固、固化于文化。多数变革失败于低估了人的抵触——技术易改,习惯难移。
def_en: A systematic approach to guiding an organisation and its people from the current state to a target state. Kotter's eight steps: create urgency, build a coalition, form a vision, communicate it, empower action, generate short-term wins, consolidate, anchor in culture. Most change efforts fail by underestimating human resistance — systems are easy to change, habits hard.

sources.chapter: Organisation, Tech & Governance 组织、科技与治理

etymology: change 源自古法语 changier（改变，拉丁 cambiare 交换）——变革管理即'管理从旧到新的交换过程'。

synonyms.sentence: Kotter's eight steps treat change as a campaign, not a memo.
The change curve tracks people from denial through resistance to commitment.

example: A transformation with a flawless plan collapsed because change management was an afterthought — the technology worked, but no one changed how they worked.

def_zh: 埃德蒙森:团队成员相信可以坦承错误、提出异议、发问求助而不会遭羞辱或惩罚的共同信念。谷歌研究发现它是高绩效团队的首要因素——因为没有它,人会隐藏问题、不敢冒险,组织便学不到、改不了。
def_en: Amy Edmondson's concept: the shared belief that team members can admit mistakes, voice disagreement and ask for help without humiliation or punishment. Google's research found it the top predictor of high-performing teams — because without it, people hide problems and avoid risk, and the organisation cannot learn or improve.

sources.chapter: Organisation, Tech & Governance 组织、科技与治理

etymology: psychological（心理的）+ safety——指人际风险上的'安全';Edmondson 1999年研究医疗团队时提出。

synonyms.sentence: A learning climate treats the reported error as data, not as a confession.
A speak-up culture surfaces the bad news early enough to still act on it.

example: On psychologically safe teams, a junior engineer flags the flaw before launch; on unsafe ones, everyone sees it and no one says it until it's a disaster.

def_zh: 通过愿景、感召、智识激发与个性化关怀,激励追随者超越自身短期利益、追求更高集体目标的领导风格——与依赖奖惩交换的'交易型领导'相对。它擅长激发变革与投入,但也可能滑向个人崇拜。
def_en: A leadership style that inspires followers to transcend their own short-term interests for higher collective goals, through vision, charisma, intellectual stimulation and individual attention — as opposed to transactional leadership, which relies on reward-and-punishment exchange. It excels at igniting change and commitment, but can slide toward a cult of personality.

sources.chapter: Organisation, Tech & Governance 组织、科技与治理

etymology: transform 源自拉丁 trans（越过）+ formare（塑形）——变革型领导即'重塑追随者的动机与目标'。

synonyms.sentence: Visionary leadership sells a future compelling enough to reorganise the present around it.
Transactional leadership trades rewards for compliance and rarely inspires more.

example: Transactional leadership gets this quarter's numbers; transformational leadership gets people to reinvent the company — and the best leaders switch between them.

def_zh: 赫塞与布兰查德:没有放之四海皆准的最佳领导风格,有效的领导者依据下属的能力与意愿,在指导型、教练型、支持型、授权型之间灵活切换。领导力不是固定人格,而是随情境调节的适配能力。
def_en: Hersey and Blanchard: there is no single best leadership style; effective leaders flex among directing, coaching, supporting and delegating according to the follower's competence and commitment. Leadership is not a fixed personality but a capacity to adapt to the situation.

sources.chapter: Organisation, Tech & Governance 组织、科技与治理

etymology: situation 源自拉丁 situs（位置、处境）——情境领导即'因处境而调整领导方式'。

synonyms.sentence: Adaptive leadership reads the follower before choosing the style.
Contingency leadership denies that one approach fits every person and moment.

example: Situational leadership means a manager directs a nervous novice closely but delegates fully to an expert — the same 'best style' would fail one of them.

def_zh: 解释人为何工作、如何被激发。马斯洛需求层次:生理→安全→归属→尊重→自我实现。赫茨伯格双因素:'保健因素'（薪资、条件）缺失致不满但存在不激励,'激励因素'（成就、认可、成长）才真正驱动投入。
def_en: Explanations of why people work and how they are energised. Maslow's hierarchy: physiological → safety → belonging → esteem → self-actualisation. Herzberg's two factors: 'hygiene factors' (pay, conditions) cause dissatisfaction if absent but don't motivate if present, while 'motivators' (achievement, recognition, growth) truly drive engagement.

sources.chapter: Organisation, Tech & Governance 组织、科技与治理

etymology: motivate 源自拉丁 movere（移动）——动机即'使人行动的内在推力',与 motive、emotion 同根。

synonyms.sentence: Maslow's hierarchy of needs says you can't inspire someone whose rent is unpaid.
Hygiene factors stop dissatisfaction; only motivators create drive.

example: Herzberg's insight reframes pay: raising a low salary removes dissatisfaction, but recognition and growth — the motivators — are what actually lift performance.

def_zh: 平克与自我决定理论:对复杂创造性工作,金钱等外在激励效果有限甚至适得其反,真正的驱动来自自主（autonomy）、精进（mastery）、目的（purpose）三者。理解它,才明白为何加薪买不到投入,而意义能。
def_en: Daniel Pink and self-determination theory: for complex, creative work, extrinsic rewards like money have limited or even perverse effects, and real drive comes from autonomy, mastery and purpose. Grasp this and you understand why a raise can't buy engagement but meaning can.

sources.chapter: Organisation, Tech & Governance 组织、科技与治理

etymology: intrinsic 源自拉丁 intrinsecus（在内部）——内在动机即'源于活动本身而非外部奖赏'的驱动力。

synonyms.sentence: Autonomy, mastery and purpose motivate the knowledge worker where the carrot and stick fail.
The overjustification effect shows extrinsic rewards can crowd out intrinsic joy.

example: Paying people more to do creative work often backfires — intrinsic motivation, not a bigger bonus, is what makes them care about the problem itself.

def_zh: 芒格箴言:'给我看激励,我就告诉你结果。'人会优化被奖励的东西——常常连带优化了你不想要的副作用。设计激励须防钻空子（度量什么就得到什么）、对齐长期利益、平衡内在与外在动机。组织行为的第一杠杆。
def_en: Munger's maxim: 'show me the incentive and I'll show you the outcome.' People optimise what is rewarded — often together with side effects you didn't want. Good incentive design guards against gaming (you get what you measure), aligns long-term interests, and balances intrinsic with extrinsic motivation. The first lever of organisational behaviour.

sources.chapter: Organisation, Tech & Governance 组织、科技与治理

etymology: incentive 源自拉丁 incentivum（起调、激起，incinere 唱起）——激励即'唤起行动之物'。

synonyms.sentence: Incentive alignment makes doing the right thing also the rewarded thing.
Perverse incentives reward the behaviour that defeats the goal.

example: A bounty on dead cobras once bred cobra farms — the classic perverse incentive, and a warning that people optimise the reward, not your intention.

def_zh: 费舍与尤里《谈判力》:不在立场上讨价还价,而是'对事不对人'、聚焦利益而非立场、创造互利选项、坚持客观标准。核心概念 BATNA（谈判协议的最佳替代方案）——你的 BATNA 越强,谈判筹码越大。
def_en: Fisher and Ury's Getting to Yes: don't haggle over positions but separate people from the problem, focus on interests not positions, invent options for mutual gain, and insist on objective criteria. The key concept is BATNA (best alternative to a negotiated agreement) — the stronger your BATNA, the more leverage you hold.

sources.chapter: Organisation, Tech & Governance 组织、科技与治理

etymology: negotiate 源自拉丁 negotium（事务,neg-不+otium 闲暇='没闲功夫、忙于事务'）——谈判即'处理事务、达成交易'。

synonyms.sentence: Your BATNA is your walk-away power; without it, you're pleading, not negotiating.
Focusing on interests, not positions, finds the deal that both hidden agendas actually want.

example: A negotiator with a strong BATNA — a real alternative — can hold firm, while one with no walk-away option is negotiating with themselves.

def_zh: 麦肯锡1997年提出:在知识经济中,顶尖人才是最稀缺、最具决定性的资源,吸引、发展与留住他们是核心竞争优势的来源。少数关键岗位上的卓越者与平庸者产出可差数倍——人才不是成本,是资本。
def_en: A McKinsey coinage (1997): in the knowledge economy, top talent is the scarcest and most decisive resource, and attracting, developing and retaining it is a source of core competitive advantage. In a few pivotal roles, an outstanding performer can out-produce an average one many times over — talent is not a cost but capital.

sources.chapter: Organisation, Tech & Governance 组织、科技与治理

etymology: war for talent——麦肯锡以'战争'之喻强调人才争夺的激烈;talent 源自希腊 talanton（重量、钱币单位，圣经寓言引申为天赋）。

synonyms.sentence: Human capital is the only asset that can walk out the door and join a competitor.
The A-player premium is why one great engineer beats three good ones.

example: In pivotal creative and technical roles, the gap between a great hire and an average one is enormous — which is why firms wage a war for talent over a handful of people.

def_zh: 麦肯锡:组织长期绩效不仅取决于'表现'（当下财务结果),更取决于'健康'——协同、执行、更新自我的能力。健康的组织有清晰方向、高质量领导、强执行文化,能持续赢;不健康的组织即便一时业绩好也难持久。
def_en: McKinsey's finding: long-term performance depends not only on 'performance' (current financial results) but on 'health' — the ability to align, execute and renew itself. Healthy organisations have clear direction, quality leadership and a strong execution culture, and keep winning; unhealthy ones can't sustain even good results.

sources.chapter: Organisation, Tech & Governance 组织、科技与治理

etymology: health 古英语 hælth（完整、健全，与 whole 同源）——组织'健康'即其作为整体的健全与自我更新能力。

synonyms.sentence: The health-performance link says how you win matters as much as whether you win.
Organisational fitness is the capacity to keep winning after this year's leaders leave.

example: Two firms hit the same targets, but organisational health predicts which survives the next shock — alignment and execution culture outlast any single quarter.

def_zh: 归于德鲁克的名言:再精妙的战略也会被与之抵触的组织文化悄悄消解。文化——共享的价值观、默认行为、'我们这里怎么做事'——决定战略能否真正落地。改战略易,改文化难,而后者往往才是胜负手。
def_en: A dictum attributed to Peter Drucker: however brilliant, a strategy is quietly digested by an organisational culture that resists it. Culture — shared values, default behaviours, 'how we do things here' — decides whether strategy actually lands. Changing strategy is easy, changing culture is hard, and the latter usually decides the outcome.

sources.chapter: Organisation, Tech & Governance 组织、科技与治理

etymology: culture 源自拉丁 colere（耕种、培育）——组织文化即'被长期培育出的共同习性';这句格言使其地位广为人知。

synonyms.word: the way we do things here
synonyms.sentence: 'The way we do things here' is culture, and it overrules the strategy deck daily.
Culture is the invisible operating system every plan must run on.

example: A bold digital strategy died on contact with a risk-averse culture that punished failure — culture ate the strategy, exactly as Drucker warned.

def_zh: 把任务与相应的决策权、责任下放给他人,是领导者规模化自身产出、发展团队、聚焦高价值工作的核心技能。授权不等于放弃责任(结果仍需担责),也不等于放任(需匹配对方能力、给清晰目标与边界)。
def_en: Handing tasks, along with the matching decision authority and responsibility, to others — a core skill for leaders to scale their own output, develop the team and focus on high-value work. Delegation is neither abdication (you remain accountable for outcomes) nor abandonment (it must match the person's capability, with clear goals and boundaries).

sources.chapter: Organisation, Tech & Governance 组织、科技与治理

etymology: delegate 源自拉丁 de-（向下）+ legare（委派）——授权即'把任务向下委派'。

synonyms.sentence: Empowerment is delegation that also hands over the authority to decide.
Abdication is delegation without accountability — and it usually ends badly.

example: A founder who won't delegate becomes the bottleneck of their own company — delegation, not heroics, is what lets an organisation outgrow one person.

def_zh: 净利润除以流通股数——每一股股票分摊到的利润。它是市盈率的分母、市场关注的核心指标,也因此成为可被操纵的对象:回购、并购、会计选择都能推高 EPS 而未必创造真实价值。看 EPS,更要问它从何而来。
def_en: Net profit divided by shares outstanding — the profit attributable to each share. It is the denominator of the P/E ratio and a headline metric markets watch, and therefore also a target for manipulation: buybacks, acquisitions and accounting choices can lift EPS without creating real value. Read EPS, but ask where it came from.

sources.chapter: Organisation, Tech & Governance 组织、科技与治理

etymology: per（每）源自拉丁'通过、按';EPS 即'按每股计算的收益',是股东视角的利润切分。

synonyms.sentence: Diluted EPS counts every option and convertible, showing the profit per fully-loaded share.
EPS growth flatters itself when buybacks, not the business, are doing the work.

example: A firm hit its EPS target by buying back shares while profit was flat — EPS rose, but the business behind each share did not.

def_zh: 用数字技术（云、数据、AI、自动化）从根本上重塑商业模式、流程与客户体验,而非仅把线下环节搬到线上。真正的转型改变'公司如何创造与捕获价值',其最大障碍往往不是技术而是组织文化与既得利益。
def_en: Using digital technology (cloud, data, AI, automation) to fundamentally reshape business models, processes and customer experience — not merely moving offline steps online. Real transformation changes how a company creates and captures value, and its biggest obstacle is usually not technology but culture and vested interests.

sources.chapter: Organisation, Tech & Governance 组织、科技与治理

etymology: digital 源自拉丁 digitus（手指——计数始于掰指头）；转型即'根本的形态改变',非表层数字化。

synonyms.sentence: Digitalisation done right rewires the business, not just its front end.
Technology-enabled reinvention is transformation; putting a PDF online is not.

example: Most digital transformations fail not on the technology but on the org: new tools bolted onto old processes and incentives change nothing.

def_zh: 以短周期迭代（冲刺 sprint）、跨职能小团队、快速反馈与持续调整取代'一次性大计划'的工作方式。源自软件开发,现扩展至各类知识工作。核心信念:面对不确定,快速试错、边做边学胜过预先完美规划。
def_en: A way of working that replaces one-big-plan with short iterative cycles (sprints), small cross-functional teams, fast feedback and continuous adjustment. Born in software, now spread across knowledge work. Its core belief: under uncertainty, rapid trial-and-error and learning by doing beat perfect upfront planning.

sources.chapter: Organisation, Tech & Governance 组织、科技与治理

etymology: agile 源自拉丁 agere（做、驱动，agilis 敏捷的）——2001年《敏捷宣言》使其成为软件与管理的运动。

synonyms.sentence: Iterative development ships something small, learns, and ships again.
Scrum organises the work into sprints so course-correction is never far away.

example: Agile teams ship a rough feature in a two-week sprint and learn from real users, rather than spending a year building the wrong thing perfectly.

def_zh: 柯林斯:卓越不是靠某个决定性时刻,而是持续朝一个方向推动一个沉重的飞轮——起初费力、几乎不动,但每一圈都积累动量,最终它靠自身惯性飞速旋转。它是自我强化的良性循环在企业战略中的隐喻。
def_en: Jim Collins: greatness comes not from a single decisive moment but from persistently pushing a heavy flywheel in one direction — at first laborious and barely moving, but each turn builds momentum until it spins fast on its own inertia. It is the metaphor for a self-reinforcing virtuous cycle in corporate strategy.

sources.chapter: Organisation, Tech & Governance 组织、科技与治理

etymology: flywheel（飞轮）——机械中储存旋转动量的重轮;柯林斯《从优秀到卓越》使其成为战略隐喻。

synonyms.sentence: A virtuous cycle feeds itself: each turn makes the next one easier.
Compounding momentum is why the flywheel that barely moved last year now can't be stopped.

example: Amazon's flywheel — lower prices bring more customers, which bring more sellers, which lower prices further — spins faster with every turn.

def_zh: 新技术在人群中扩散的钟形曲线:创新者→早期采用者→早期大众→晚期大众→落后者。每一群人的心理与购买动机不同,营销须逐段调整。它是理解创新扩散、时机与规模化的基础模型。
def_en: The bell-curve by which a new technology spreads through a population: innovators → early adopters → early majority → late majority → laggards. Each group differs in psychology and buying motive, so marketing must adapt stage by stage. A foundational model for understanding diffusion, timing and scaling.

sources.chapter: Organisation, Tech & Governance 组织、科技与治理

etymology: 由 Everett Rogers《创新的扩散》(1962) 奠基——把创新在社会中的传播画成一条钟形曲线。

synonyms.sentence: The diffusion of innovations explains why the same product needs different pitches over its life.
Early adopters buy the vision; the early majority buys the proof.

example: A gadget beloved by innovators and early adopters can still fail — unless it crosses the chasm to the pragmatic early majority who want references, not novelty.

def_zh: 摩尔:在早期采用者（追求领先、容忍缺陷）与早期大众（务实、要成熟方案与参照客户）之间,存在一道致命的'鸿沟'。许多创新在此夭折。跨越之道:聚焦一个细分'滩头阵地',做到全面解决方案,再向邻近市场扩张。
def_en: Geoffrey Moore: between early adopters (who chase the edge and tolerate flaws) and the early majority (pragmatists who want proven solutions and reference customers) lies a deadly 'chasm' where many innovations die. To cross it: focus on one 'beachhead' niche, deliver a whole product, then expand to adjacent markets.

sources.chapter: Organisation, Tech & Governance 组织、科技与治理

etymology: chasm（鸿沟，希腊 khasma 裂口）——摩尔1991年同名书指采用曲线上早期与主流之间的致命断层。

synonyms.sentence: The beachhead market is the narrow niche you dominate before invading the mainland.
The whole product is everything the pragmatic buyer needs to say yes, not just the core.

collocations: from early adopters to the majority
example: A startup adored by tech enthusiasts stalled at the chasm — it won by narrowing to one industry, nailing the whole product, then expanding outward.

def_zh: 英特尔创始人摩尔1965年观察:集成电路上的晶体管数量约每两年翻一番,计算能力指数级增长而成本骤降。它既是半导体行业的自我实现预言,也是过去半世纪数字革命的底层引擎——指数增长如何重塑一切的范本。
def_en: Intel co-founder Gordon Moore's 1965 observation that the number of transistors on a chip roughly doubles every two years — computing power growing exponentially as cost collapses. Both a self-fulfilling prophecy for the semiconductor industry and the underlying engine of the digital revolution — the exemplar of how exponential growth reshapes everything.

sources.chapter: Organisation, Tech & Governance 组织、科技与治理

etymology: 以 Gordon Moore 命名——非物理定律而是经验趋势,却因成为行业目标而自我实现数十年。

synonyms.sentence: Exponential improvement is why last decade's supercomputer fits in your pocket.
The cost collapse Moore's law describes is what made computing ubiquitous.

example: Moore's law turned computing from a room-sized luxury into a disposable commodity — the exponential curve behind smartphones, cloud and AI alike.

def_zh: 产品用得越多,积累的数据越多,算法越聪明,产品越好用,又吸引更多使用——数据驱动的自增强飞轮。它是 AI 时代的新护城河:领先者的数据优势会自我放大,后来者难以追赶。搜索、推荐、自动驾驶皆靠它。
def_en: The more a product is used, the more data it accumulates, the smarter its algorithms, the better the product, the more it is used — a data-driven self-reinforcing flywheel. It is the new moat of the AI era: a leader's data advantage compounds, and latecomers struggle to catch up. Search, recommendation and self-driving all run on it.

sources.chapter: Organisation, Tech & Governance 组织、科技与治理

etymology: 结合 data（数据）与 network effect（网络效应）——但机制不同:价值来自数据积累而非用户互联。

synonyms.sentence: The data flywheel turns usage into intelligence into more usage.
A learning advantage compounds silently: every user makes the model harder to beat.

example: A search engine improves with every query — the data network effect means its lead widens with use, a moat no clever newcomer can bootstrap.

def_zh: 用机器、软件与 AI 替代或增强人力,以更少投入产出更多——生产率提升是长期财富增长的根本来源。它引发'技术性失业'的忧虑,但历史上自动化多改变而非消灭工作总量,创造新岗位。核心问题是过渡的痛苦与分配。
def_en: Using machines, software and AI to replace or augment human labour, producing more from less — productivity growth is the ultimate source of long-run wealth. It stokes fears of 'technological unemployment', but historically automation has changed rather than eliminated the total quantity of work, creating new jobs. The real issues are the pain of transition and the distribution of gains.

sources.chapter: Organisation, Tech & Governance 组织、科技与治理

etymology: automation 源自希腊 automatos（自行运作的）——自动化即'让机器自行完成';productivity 即'单位投入的产出'。

synonyms.sentence: The productivity frontier is how much output a given input can yield with the best methods.
Technological unemployment is the transition's real cost, not the end-state prophesied.

example: Automation displaced bank tellers, yet employed more of them — ATMs cut the cost per branch, so banks opened more branches, until digital changed the game again.

def_zh: 从环境、社会责任、公司治理三维度评估企业可持续性与非财务风险的框架。支持者视其为长期风险管理与价值创造;批评者指其标准混乱、易沦为'漂绿'、并牵扯政治。无论立场,它已深刻影响资本配置与企业战略。
def_en: A framework for assessing a company's sustainability and non-financial risk across three dimensions: environmental, social and governance. Supporters see it as long-term risk management and value creation; critics point to inconsistent standards, 'greenwashing' and political entanglement. Either way, it now shapes capital allocation and corporate strategy profoundly.

sources.chapter: Organisation, Tech & Governance 组织、科技与治理

etymology: Environmental/Social/Governance 首字母缩略——2004年联合国报告普及,成为可持续投资的通用语。

synonyms.sentence: Sustainable investing bets that ignoring these risks is the truly imprudent stance.
Greenwashing is ESG as marketing rather than management.

example: ESG began as risk management — a factory ignoring its emissions faces future liability — but sprawled into a contested label critics call vague and gameable.

def_zh: 给碳排放定价以矫正气候这一负外部性的市场机制:碳税（直接定价）或总量管制与交易（cap-and-trade,设排放上限、发放可交易配额）。核心思想是让污染者承担其社会成本,用价格信号引导减排到成本最低处。
def_en: Market mechanisms that price carbon emissions to correct the climate negative externality: a carbon tax (pricing directly) or cap-and-trade (setting an emissions ceiling and issuing tradable allowances). The core idea is to make polluters bear their social cost and let the price signal steer cuts to where they are cheapest.

sources.chapter: Organisation, Tech & Governance 组织、科技与治理

etymology: carbon（碳）+ market——把'排放权'商品化以交易;cap-and-trade 即'限额与交易'。

synonyms.sentence: Cap-and-trade fixes the quantity of emissions and lets the market find the price.
The carbon price makes the atmosphere's cost visible on the balance sheet at last.

example: A carbon price makes the polluter's hidden cost explicit — the cheapest emissions cuts happen first, exactly as the externality theory predicts.

def_zh: 系统性地识别、评估、排序与应对威胁企业目标的不确定性:规避、降低、转移（保险/对冲）或承受。核心不是消除所有风险（不可能且无回报),而是承担有回报的风险、对冲无回报的风险,并为尾部事件留出冗余。
def_en: Systematically identifying, assessing, prioritising and responding to the uncertainties that threaten a firm's objectives: avoid, reduce, transfer (insure/hedge) or accept. The point is not to eliminate all risk (impossible and unrewarded) but to take rewarded risks, hedge unrewarded ones, and hold slack for tail events.

sources.chapter: Organisation, Tech & Governance 组织、科技与治理

etymology: risk 源自意大利 risco（触礁之险）——风险管理即'为组织的航行避礁、备险'。

synonyms.sentence: Risk appetite defines which dangers a firm is paid to bear and which it is not.
A risk register lists the threats so they can be owned rather than merely feared.

example: Good risk management doesn't mean taking no risk — it means an airline hedges fuel (unrewarded risk) while betting on routes (rewarded risk).

def_zh: 把分散在各部门的风险管理提升为全企业统一、自上而下的体系,识别相互关联、可能叠加的风险,并与战略和资本配置挂钩。它防止'各扫门前雪'式的孤岛管理漏掉那些跨部门累积、足以致命的系统性风险。
def_en: Elevating siloed, departmental risk management into a firm-wide, top-down system that identifies interconnected and compounding risks and ties them to strategy and capital allocation. It prevents the fragmented, everyone-guards-their-own-patch approach from missing the cross-cutting, accumulating risks that can prove fatal.

sources.chapter: Organisation, Tech & Governance 组织、科技与治理

etymology: enterprise（企业整体）——把风险管理从部门层面提升到'全企业'视角,故名 ERM。

synonyms.sentence: A risk-aware culture spots the correlated exposures a siloed one never assembles.
The three lines of defence separate risk-taking, risk oversight and independent audit.

example: Siloed risk management saw each exposure as small; enterprise risk management revealed they were all correlated to one factor — and together, existential.

def_zh: 用一个数字概括投资组合的风险:在给定置信度（如95%）与时间窗（如一天）内,可能发生的最大损失。它让风险可量化、可比较、可管理,但致命缺陷是对'尾部'（罕见极端事件）估计不足——它告诉你正常日子的坏,却低估灾难日的糟。
def_en: Summarising a portfolio's risk in one number: the maximum loss expected over a given horizon (e.g. a day) at a given confidence level (e.g. 95%). It makes risk quantifiable, comparable and manageable — but its fatal flaw is under-estimating the tail: it tells you about a bad normal day and understates the catastrophic one.

sources.chapter: Organisation, Tech & Governance 组织、科技与治理

etymology: value at risk（处于风险中的价值）——1990年代 J.P. Morgan 推广,成为银行风险度量的行业标准。

synonyms.sentence: The tail beyond VaR is exactly where the crises live.
Expected shortfall asks how bad the loss is when VaR is breached, patching one of its holes.

example: A bank's VaR said it could lose at most 50m on a bad day — then a once-in-a-century event lost it billions, exposing the model's blind spot in the tail.
nuance: VaR 的危险在于制造虚假安全感:它给出一个精确数字,却系统性低估极端事件——'95%的日子安全'恰恰掩盖了那毁灭性的5%。

def_zh: 塔勒布:极罕见、事前几乎不可预测、事后却被强行合理化、影响却极其巨大的事件——2008金融危机、疫情。核心教训:不要押注于'预测'这类事件（做不到),而要构建能在冲击中幸存甚至受益的稳健(乃至反脆弱)系统。
def_en: Nassim Taleb's term for events that are extremely rare, nearly unpredictable in advance, rationalised in hindsight, yet enormous in impact — the 2008 crisis, the pandemic. The lesson: don't bet on predicting such events (you can't), but build robust — even antifragile — systems that survive, or gain from, the shock.

sources.chapter: Organisation, Tech & Governance 组织、科技与治理

etymology: 源自旧欧洲谚语'黑天鹅般不可能',直到在澳洲发现黑天鹅——喻'被认定不可能却真实发生'之事;塔勒布同名书使其风行。

synonyms.sentence: A tail event is rare, ruinous, and obvious only after it arrives.
The unknown unknown is the risk you didn't even know to model.

example: Models that assumed a black-swan crash 'couldn't happen in a million years' were wrong within a decade — the tail is fatter than the bell curve admits.

def_zh: 塔勒布造词:超越'坚固'（robust,在冲击中不受损)的更高境界——反脆弱系统能从波动、压力与混乱中受益、变得更强,如肌肉因训练而强壮、免疫系统因暴露而增强。它主张拥抱适度的波动与试错,而非追求虚假的稳定。
def_en: Taleb's coinage for a property beyond robustness (which merely withstands shocks): antifragile systems gain from volatility, stress and disorder, growing stronger — like muscle from training or immunity from exposure. It argues for embracing measured volatility and trial-and-error rather than chasing a false stability.

sources.chapter: Organisation, Tech & Governance 组织、科技与治理

etymology: anti-（反）+ fragile（脆弱,拉丁 frangere 打破）——塔勒布造词:不是'不碎',而是'越受压越强'。

synonyms.sentence: Gains from disorder describe the trader, the startup and the immune system alike.
Optionality — small capped losses, large uncapped gains — is how you build antifragility.

example: An antifragile portfolio — mostly safe assets plus small bets with unlimited upside — doesn't just survive a crash, it profits from the chaos.

def_zh: 单个机构的倒闭通过相互关联（借贷、衍生品、信心）引发连锁反应、危及整个金融体系乃至实体经济的风险——2008年雷曼倒闭即引爆点。它无法靠分散化消除（因为它同时打击所有资产),是监管与央行'最后贷款人'职能存在的根本理由。
def_en: The risk that one institution's failure, through interconnection (lending, derivatives, confidence), triggers a chain reaction endangering the whole financial system and the real economy — Lehman's 2008 collapse being the trigger. It cannot be diversified away (it hits all assets at once) and is the fundamental reason for regulation and the central bank's lender-of-last-resort role.

sources.chapter: Organisation, Tech & Governance 组织、科技与治理

etymology: systemic（系统性的）——风险波及'整个系统'而非单个机构;与 systematic（系统性/市场性）近而不同。

synonyms.sentence: Financial contagion spreads panic faster than any fundamental justifies.
Interconnectedness turns one bank's problem into everyone's crisis.

example: Systemic risk is why one bank's failure can freeze the whole system — interconnection means the panic, not just the loss, propagates.

def_zh: 某些金融机构规模太大、关联太深,其倒闭将拖垮整个系统,以致政府不得不用纳税人的钱救助。这制造了严重的道德风险:机构因预期被救而敢冒更大风险——收益私有化,亏损社会化。2008后监管的核心难题。
def_en: Some financial institutions are so large and interconnected that their failure would bring down the whole system, forcing governments to bail them out with taxpayers' money. This creates severe moral hazard: institutions take bigger risks expecting rescue — profits privatised, losses socialised. The central regulatory dilemma after 2008.

sources.chapter: Organisation, Tech & Governance 组织、科技与治理

pron: too big to fail /tu bɪg tɪ feɪl/
etymology: too big to fail（大到不能让它倒）——1984年美国国会听证首次用于银行救助,2008年后成为全球政策核心词。

synonyms.sentence: The implicit guarantee lets giants borrow cheaply because the state will catch them.
'Privatise gains, socialise losses' is the moral hazard in one phrase.

example: Bailing out a 'too big to fail' bank averts collapse but rewards recklessness — the rescued institution learns that its downside is the public's problem.

def_zh: 当一方无须承担其冒险行为的全部后果时,就会承担过度风险。存款保险、央行救助、'大而不能倒'的隐性担保都会滋生它:银行明知有兜底而放贷更激进。它是金融监管永恒的两难——既要防危机,又不能鼓励鲁莽。
def_en: When a party doesn't bear the full consequences of its risk-taking, it takes on too much risk. Deposit insurance, central-bank rescues and the implicit 'too big to fail' guarantee all breed it: banks lend more recklessly knowing they're backstopped. It is the perpetual dilemma of financial regulation — preventing crises without rewarding recklessness.

sources.chapter: Organisation, Tech & Governance 组织、科技与治理

etymology: 见 information-asymmetry 节点;moral hazard 源自19世纪保险业行话,hazard 词根为阿拉伯'骰子'。

synonyms.sentence: The backstop problem is that a safety net can make the acrobat careless.

synonyms.word: heads I win, tails you lose
synonyms.sentence: 'Heads I win, tails you lose' is moral hazard from the risk-taker's chair.

example: Guaranteeing bank deposits prevents runs but breeds moral hazard — so regulators pair the safety net with capital rules to keep bankers cautious.

def_zh: 全球银行监管标准,要求银行按其资产的风险持有最低比例的自有资本作缓冲,以吸收损失、防止倒闭。核心逻辑:资本是抵御亏损的第一道防线,资本越薄、杠杆越高,一点损失就足以让银行资不抵债。2008后不断收紧。
def_en: Global banking standards requiring banks to hold a minimum ratio of their own capital against their risk-weighted assets as a buffer to absorb losses and prevent failure. The core logic: capital is the first line of defence against loss, and the thinner the capital (the higher the leverage), the smaller the loss needed to render a bank insolvent. Tightened repeatedly since 2008.

sources.chapter: Organisation, Tech & Governance 组织、科技与治理

etymology: 以瑞士巴塞尔市（国际清算银行所在地）命名——Basel I/II/III 是全球银行监管的历代协议。

synonyms.sentence: Capital adequacy is how much loss a bank can eat before it fails.
The capital buffer is the shock absorber between a bad year and a bank run.

example: Higher Basel capital requirements make banks safer but lending dearer — the eternal trade-off between financial stability and credit growth.

def_zh: 经济繁荣时,乐观情绪与宽松信贷推动债务与资产价格螺旋上升(加杠杆);一旦逆转,抛售还债引发资产价格暴跌、进一步逼迫去杠杆,酿成危机。明斯基:'稳定本身孕育不稳定'——繁荣的种子里长出崩溃。
def_en: In a boom, optimism and easy credit spiral debt and asset prices upward (leveraging up); once it reverses, selling to repay debt crashes asset prices, forcing further deleveraging into crisis. Minsky: 'stability itself is destabilising' — the crash grows from the seeds of the boom.

sources.chapter: Organisation, Tech & Governance 组织、科技与治理

etymology: leverage（杠杆）+ cycle——债务的扩张与收缩周期;Minsky 与 Geanakoplos 的理论核心。

synonyms.sentence: The Minsky moment is when the debt built in good times suddenly demands repayment.
The debt supercycle is the leverage cycle stretched across decades.

example: The 2008 crisis was a leverage cycle in the housing market: cheap credit inflated prices until a small fall forced deleveraging that crashed everything.

def_zh: 过度依赖数学模型带来的风险:模型的假设在现实(尤其极端情况)中失效,却因其精确的数字外表而被误信。'所有模型都是错的,有些是有用的'——危险在于忘记前半句。LTCM 崩盘、2008危机皆有模型风险之影。
def_en: The risk from over-relying on mathematical models: their assumptions fail in reality (especially in extremes), yet are trusted because of the precise numbers they output. 'All models are wrong, but some are useful' — the danger is forgetting the first half. The LTCM collapse and the 2008 crisis both bear the fingerprints of model risk.

sources.chapter: Organisation, Tech & Governance 组织、科技与治理

etymology: model 源自拉丁 modulus（小尺度、标准）——模型即'现实的简化尺度';模型风险即'误把地图当领土'。

synonyms.word: the map is not the territory
synonyms.sentence: 'The map is not the territory' is model risk stated as philosophy.
Spurious precision is a wrong answer dressed in three decimal places.

collocations: the map is not the territory
example: Traders trusted a model that assumed markets never jump; when they jumped, the precise-looking numbers were precisely wrong, and the fund blew up.

def_zh: 一国政府无力或不愿偿还其债务的风险。与企业不同,主权国家难以被'清算',违约多是政治选择与经济能力的混合。它推高该国借贷成本、引发货币贬值与资本外逃,严重时酿成主权债务危机(希腊、阿根廷)。
def_en: The risk that a national government cannot or will not repay its debt. Unlike a company, a sovereign can't easily be 'liquidated', so default is a mix of political choice and economic capacity. It raises the country's borrowing costs, triggers currency depreciation and capital flight, and in severe cases a sovereign debt crisis (Greece, Argentina).

sources.chapter: Organisation, Tech & Governance 组织、科技与治理

etymology: sovereign 见 westphalia 节点(拉丁 super 至上)——主权风险即'至高无上的国家也可能赖账'的风险。

synonyms.sentence: Country risk bundles a government's willingness and ability to honour its word.
